ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Prawn Balchao; closest defensible anchor is 'shrimp dish' (FOODON_03311737). Goan pickling-style preparation is not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for spiced cooked prawn preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Prawn Balchao. A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible match. Pickling-style spice reduction is not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Prawn Balchao or equivalent Goan prawn pickle preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Prawn Balchao is approximately 150 g, typically consumed as a side dish alongside plain steamed rice or Goan Poi bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Prawn Balchao are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using cooked prawn and spice-paste composition data. No exact Prawn Balchao entry exists in the database; values are estimated from cooked prawn base (ICMR-NIN) with adjustments for oil, vinegar, and spice additions consistent with standard Goan Balchao recipes. Saturated fat estimated from cooking oil contribution (~2.5 g/100 g) consistent with USDA FoodData Central coconut/vegetable oil data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Seafood and Spiced Preparations — analogous estimate based on cooked prawn and vinegar-acidified low-carbohydrate dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); supported by: Liljeberg H, Björck I. Delayed gastric emptying rate may explain improved glycaemia in healthy subjects to a starchy meal with added vinegar. European Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 1998.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawn Balchao. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on: (1) prawns are a zero-GI protein food; (2) the carbohydrate content is low (6.2 g/100 g), primarily from spices and small amounts of sugar; (3) acetic acid from vinegar is well-documented to reduce postprandial glycemic response by slowing gastric emptying (Liljeberg & Björck, 1998, Eur J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (30 × 6.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.79, rounded to 2.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg/100 g) from added salt and pickling process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Prawn Balchao to small portions (≤75 g) and request reduced-salt preparation, as a standard 150 g serving delivers approximately 630 mg sodium — over 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~30) and high protein content (14.5 g/100 g); vinegar acidity slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn Balchao is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 150 g serving alongside low-GI accompaniments such as steamed rice in controlled portions, as the dish itself contributes minimal glycemic load (~2.8 GL per serving).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (420 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (10.8 g/100 g); prawns provide beneficial selenium and omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Prawn Balchao should be consumed in moderation (≤100 g per occasion) with attention to sodium intake; the prawn-derived selenium (38.5 mcg/100 g) and omega-3 content offer cardioprotective benefits that partially offset the sodium and fat concerns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Prawns provide omega-3 fatty acids and selenium with anti-inflammatory properties; high spice and vinegar content may irritate sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Prawn Balchao may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis for its omega-3 and selenium content, but those sensitive to high-acidity or capsaicin-rich foods should reduce portion size or frequency to avoid gastrointestinal irritation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Balchao is a fiery, tangy, and intensely spiced Goan dish rooted in the Indo-Portuguese culinary tradition of Goa, India. It is prepared by grinding dried red chilies, cinnamon, cloves, black peppercorns, garlic, and ginger with malt or toddy vinegar to form a concentrated 'Balchao' masala paste. Fresh prawns are first sautéed in hot oil, then slow-cooked in this paste until the oil separates and the sauce reduces to a thick, pickle-like consistency. The result is a dish with a deep-red colour, sharp acidity, and intense heat that closely resembles a prawn pickle and can be preserved for several days. Prawn Balchao is traditionally served as a side dish with plain steamed rice or Goan Poi (local bread) at lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 175 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 10.8 g fat, and 6.2 g carbohydrate, with a notably high selenium content (38.5 mcg) and a significant sodium level (420 mg) from the pickling process. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in controlled portions. The vinegar content contributes to a reduced postprandial glycemic response. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and should be used cautiously by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while its low GI and high protein make it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free.

meal_context: travel food due to pickling preservation

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data with recipe-based adjustments for oil, vinegar, and spice additions. No exact Prawn Balchao entry exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate supported by peer-reviewed evidence on vinegar and low-carbohydrate seafood dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct database entry for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using cooked prawn base with recipe-level adjustments; no exact Prawn Balchao entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 2.5 g/100 g based on cooking oil contribution (coconut or vegetable oil, ~1–2 tbsp per 200 g prawns) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central oil data. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific Goan preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 4. cholesterol_mg set to -1: cholesterol data for this specific preparation not reported in ICMR-NIN; USDA data for cooked shrimp (~189 mg/100 g raw) may be used as a reference but is not directly applied here due to preparation differences. 5. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this pickling-style preparation not reported in available approved sources. 6. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Prawn Balchao exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The alias language tag 'ko' in the input JSON for 'सुंगटांचे बालचांव' and 'Sungtanche Balchao' appears to be an error — these are Konkani-language forms, not Korean. Corrected to 'konkani' in aliases_by_language. Similarly, 'po' for Portuguese has been corrected to 'portuguese'. 8. Duplicate portion entries in input (four identical 150 g entries) consolidated to a single default serving of 150 g. 9. trans_fat_g set to 0.0 as this preparation uses natural cooking oils with no industrial trans-fat sources.
